title: Unexpected Company

jokes.text: Why do we have to have a party every time someone in this family is released from prison? / Tradition.
jokes.setup: Question posed earnestly about the frequency of prison-release parties, implying it happens often enough to be tiresome.
jokes.punchline: One-word answer 'Tradition' — delivered deadpan, treating something absurd as completely normal.
jokes.explanation: The one-word punchline 'Tradition' is perfectly economical and perfectly Bluth — treating criminality as a wholesome family ritual. The structure is tight and the deadpan delivery implied is characteristically Lucille.

jokes.text: Like that god-awful thing you used to make me do on your birthday. / Cook?
jokes.setup: George Sr. implies Lucille used to do something 'god-awful' on his birthday, implying a sexual favor.
jokes.punchline: Lucille's guess of 'Cook?' subverts the sexual implication and reveals that cooking was the objectionable task — itself a joke about Lucille's domestic uselessness.
jokes.explanation: Beautifully constructed misdirection. The setup baits a sexual implication, then Lucille's 'Cook?' subverts it twice: once by being non-sexual, and again because cooking being 'god-awful' perfectly encapsulates Lucille's character. Could only be Lucille. Economy is exceptional — two words carry enormous weight.

jokes.text: Not in front of the grandkids. I don't want them to know about the shady business scams that go on in this family. / We lied to investors, so we have no product and we're two million dollars in debt.
jokes.setup: Lucille shushes discussion of family crimes 'not in front of the grandkids.'
jokes.punchline: Michael immediately and bluntly summarizes the crimes in full detail, undercutting Lucille's feigned propriety.
jokes.explanation: Classic AD structure: Lucille's hypocritical propriety punctured by blunt reality. Works well, though it's a familiar pattern for the show.

jokes.text: The next welcome home party's gonna be for us. / Oh, don't be ridiculous. My name isn't on anything.
jokes.setup: Michael warns that the family's crimes mean they could end up in prison.
jokes.punchline: Lucille's response isn't denial of wrongdoing — it's relief that she covered her paper trail. Perfectly in character.
jokes.explanation: Lucille's complete lack of moral concern — her only worry is legal exposure — is pitch-perfect character comedy. 'My name isn't on anything' is a great line because it reveals character instantly.

jokes.text: Know what? I've never been attracted to handsome men.
jokes.setup: George Michael is anxious about Rebel potentially being interested in another man.
jokes.punchline: Rebel reassures him by confessing she's not attracted to handsome men — inadvertently insulting George Michael.
jokes.explanation: Clean double-edged compliment/insult structure. The punchline does double duty: reassures him about the other man and inadvertently puts George Michael down. Good economy.

jokes.text: We've just been busy with all our coding and all the goddamn... coding and, um... also programming.
jokes.setup: Rebel wants to see Fakeblock, which doesn't exist. George Michael has to improvise an excuse.
jokes.punchline: His excuse is hilariously thin — he just repeats 'coding' twice and then adds 'programming,' a synonym, as if that helps.
jokes.explanation: The escalating repetition of 'coding' mirrors a real panic-lie in a painfully recognizable way. George Michael's nervous incompetence at deception is core to his character. The addition of 'also programming' as a desperate synonym is the perfect comic beat.

jokes.text: So stop having feelings for her. / What? Is that something you can do with people?
jokes.setup: George Michael says he can't deceive Rebel because he has feelings for her. Lucille dismisses this with a command.
jokes.punchline: George Michael's genuine confusion — 'Is that something you can do with people?' — reveals how emotionally healthy he is by Bluth standards, i.e., pathologically sincere.
jokes.explanation: Excellent joke. George Michael's bewildered sincerity vs. Lucille's sociopathic emotional pragmatism is the core dynamic of the show. 'Is that something you can do with people?' is highly quotable and reveals character beautifully. Works on its own and sets up the 'heart switch' bit.

jokes.text: Yeah, once I learned how to do it with my parents, it was easy with everyone else. It's like a heart switch. Click. I love you. Click. I love you not.
jokes.setup: Lucille explains how she turns off feelings for people as a coping mechanism.
jokes.punchline: The 'heart switch' metaphor delivered with complete sincerity, and applied first to her own parents, is darkly revealing of her sociopathic emotional life.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's strongest jokes. The 'heart switch' concept is brilliant original character writing — it perfectly encapsulates Lucille's emotional psychopathy while being genuinely funny. 'Click. I love you. Click. I love you not.' is rhythmically perfect and instantly quotable. The detail that she learned it from her OWN parents first adds a horrifying layer. This is close to the 8.0 anchor — sharp, character-specific, quotable.

jokes.text: No, but in my defense, I'm not a sociopath.
jokes.setup: Lucille asks if George Michael can also turn off his feelings at will.
jokes.punchline: George Michael's response that he can't, 'in his defense,' acknowledges with resigned self-awareness that the Bluth family baseline is sociopathy.
jokes.explanation: The phrase 'in my defense' is the perfect word to hang the joke on — he's apologetically claiming normalcy as an excuse. Extremely economical and quotable. Pairs perfectly with the heart switch setup. The self-awareness without self-pity is classic George Michael.

jokes.text: Click. [George Sr. turns off feelings for George Michael]
jokes.setup: Lucille just described the 'click' heart switch method. George Sr. is shown dismissing Maeby/George Michael.
jokes.punchline: The narrator says 'Click.' — George Sr. has already applied the technique to his son, mid-conversation.
jokes.explanation: Brilliant callback. Immediately after Lucille describes the concept, the narrator uses it as a punctuation mark when George Sr. dismisses his son. The economy is near-perfect — one word does everything. The cruelty is funny because it's so quick. This is how AD uses callbacks at their best: not just a reference, but a structural payoff.

jokes.text: Gob still had a very uncomfortable 'heart on' for his friend Tony.
jokes.setup: The narrator describes Gob's lingering feelings for Tony Wonder.
jokes.punchline: 'Heart on' is a play on 'hard on' — Gob's feelings are both emotional AND physical, filtered through the 'heart switch' language just established.
jokes.explanation: Exceptional wordplay. 'Heart on' works as both a callback to the heart switch metaphor AND a crude sexual pun simultaneously, while being character-specific to Gob's confused sexuality. The narrator delivering it with perfect deadpan is essential. This is the kind of layered joke AD is famous for — multiple meanings in two words.

jokes.text: I mean, if he really is gay, he can try to hide it, but trust me, it will come out in the most unexpected balls.
jokes.setup: Gob is talking about whether Tony Wonder is really gay and whether he'll hide it.
jokes.punchline: 'Unexpected balls' instead of 'unexpected places' — a malapropism that also resonates with the running 'balls' thread and Gob's subconscious gay panic.
jokes.explanation: Great inadvertent wordplay — Gob's Freudian slip of 'balls' instead of 'places' reveals his subconscious preoccupation. Feeds naturally into the 'balls' counting gag that follows.

jokes.text: Excuse me, 'balls'? You realize you just said 'balls'? / But you said balls. / Oh, well, that's three times I'm hearing balls now.
jokes.setup: Gob accidentally says 'balls' in an inappropriate context.
jokes.punchline: The meta-counting of 'balls' utterances — each time someone points it out, they say it again, escalating the number and the absurdity.
jokes.explanation: The self-referential escalation works beautifully — each attempt to call out 'balls' adds to the count. Well-structured escalating callback. The joke is structurally clever even if the content is juvenile.

jokes.text: That's the sign of a healthy business. It's closed in the middle of a Tuesday, and the owner's locked out.
jokes.setup: George Michael proposes that if Rebel shows up at Fakeblock and it's locked, they can claim it's normal.
jokes.punchline: His sarcastic description of what that would signal — a closed office midweek, owner locked out — as a 'healthy business' is delivered with devastating irony.
jokes.explanation: Solid, self-aware absurdist joke. The specificity ('middle of a Tuesday') makes it land. Not groundbreaking but well constructed.

jokes.text: Oh, shit. That's who the note was from.
jokes.setup: Gob explains Anonymous as the Guy Fawkes mask hackers.
jokes.punchline: George Michael realizes the threatening notes he'd been receiving from 'Anonymous' were from actual Anonymous (hackers), not from Rebel as he'd assumed. A multilayered misunderstanding is resolved.
jokes.explanation: A classic AD structural payoff — the audience sees the reveal of a long-running confusion. The joke rewards attention and rewatch. The structure is tight, though it requires context from prior episodes.

jokes.text: Mom, don't say my name! [David/Anonymous member breaks cover]
jokes.setup: Anonymous hackers are delivering a threatening message to George Michael.
jokes.punchline: Anonymous member's mom calls him by name during the ominous warning, completely breaking the mystique.
jokes.explanation: Excellent deflation of Anonymous mystique — the scary hacker collective is revealed to be guys whose moms are calling them for dinner. The delivery via the narrator's description ('But Anonymous failed to be specific enough to make an impression') adds another layer of comedic understatement.

jokes.text: I understand you've ignored my cousin Kevin's... I mean, Anonymous' other warning.
jokes.setup: Anonymous member tries to maintain threatening persona.
jokes.punchline: Accidentally refers to 'cousin Kevin' before correcting to 'Anonymous,' revealing the tight-knit, family-based nature of this supposedly shadowy hacker group.
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of the Anonymous-as-dorky-family-members bit. 'Cousin Kevin' is a great specific detail. Works as a running reveal.

jokes.text: Hey, that's perfect. Those guys will ruin your life. Hey, where did you find those guys?
jokes.setup: George Michael is terrified that Anonymous is threatening him.
jokes.punchline: Gob immediately pivots from acknowledgment of the threat to asking where to hire them — he wants them as paid protesters for his own scheme.
jokes.explanation: Classic Gob — tone-deaf pivot that serves his own interests. The whiplash from 'they'll ruin your life' to 'where did you find them?' is perfectly Gob and structurally very tight.

jokes.text: Gay Gob's funny.
jokes.setup: Gob has just made his oblivious pivot request.
jokes.punchline: George Michael's aside — 'Gay Gob's funny' — breaks the fourth wall slightly and comments on the comic value of Gob's current arc.
jokes.explanation: Perfectly timed meta-comment. Three words that function as genuine character observation, meta-commentary, AND a laugh line. The economy is outstanding. It's simultaneously George Michael acknowledging something true and the writers winking at the audience.

jokes.text: You bet your balls I can get you some Guy Fawkeses. / Don't hold your balls. I would not hold my balls on that one.
jokes.setup: Gob is asked if he can get Guy Fawkes-masked protesters.
jokes.punchline: Gob uses 'balls' naturally in idiom, which by now is a running gag; 'Don't hold your balls' is a mangled 'don't hold your breath,' adding another layer.
jokes.explanation: Good extension of the balls gag with the added bonus of Gob mangling the idiom to 'Don't hold your balls' — which works both as a continuation and as a standalone character joke (Gob's obliviousness to what he's saying).

jokes.text: Oh, speaking of maniacs, let's watch this again.
jokes.setup: The family is discussing the prison break parade footage.
jokes.punchline: Lucille's casual 'speaking of maniacs' suggests she regularly classifies members of her family as maniacs, and her eagerness to rewatch is gleeful rather than alarmed.
jokes.explanation: Small but effective Lucille character moment. Her delight in watching the chaos is perfectly in character.

jokes.text: Well, it's not you! [responding to 'Is that Oscar?']
jokes.setup: Family watches video of the prison break. Someone asks if the person is Oscar (George Sr.'s twin).
jokes.punchline: Lucille's 'Well, it's not you!' to George Sr. is a dig about his inability to do anything physical/heroic, while also implying she can tell the twins apart by competence level.
jokes.explanation: Brilliant economy. Four words that simultaneously identify Oscar, insult George Sr., and reveal Lucille's contempt. The joke is richer if you know the George/Oscar twin dynamic.

jokes.text: Buster's in jail? My boy. My boy.
jokes.setup: Oscar is told Buster is in jail (after being told to warn George that cops want the stair car evidence)
jokes.punchline: Oscar's 'my boy' — delivered with confused paternal love — lands as a joke because Oscar is Buster's biological father but has never acted as one, and also because the response is completely backwards (Buster being in jail isn't the crisis Oscar was sent to address)
jokes.explanation: The 'my boy' resonates more for viewers who know Oscar is Buster's real father — rewatch bonus. As a standalone beat it's mild.

jokes.text: So they escaped from prison a half hour before he was gonna be legally released?
jokes.setup: Oscar broke Buster out of prison in a dramatic jailbreak.
jokes.punchline: Michael's flat summary of the situation highlights the absurd timing — they went to all that trouble for nothing.
jokes.explanation: Solid observational joke — the irony of the dramatic prison break being entirely unnecessary is the joke. Michael as straight man works well here.

jokes.text: He's got a lot of spunk. I like that on a guy.
jokes.setup: Lucille comments admiringly on Oscar's prison break.
jokes.punchline: 'I like that on a guy' transforms 'spunk' from its figurative use (boldness) to a crude sexual double entendre, delivered with Lucille's complete deadpan.
jokes.explanation: The 'spunk' double entendre is familiar territory, but Lucille's delivery and the context (admiring the man she cheated with/is cheating on her husband with) give it some added flavor.

jokes.text: Or worse, she could come back, and they would charge us with embezzlement.
jokes.setup: Discussion of Lucille 2 possibly being dead — clearly that would be 'bad.'
jokes.punchline: Lucille's 'or worse' pivot reveals that from her perspective, Lucille 2 returning alive is worse than Lucille 2 being dead — because it means an embezzlement charge rather than a murder charge.
jokes.explanation: Superb dark joke. The inversion — alive is worse than dead — and the specificity of Lucille's legal calculus make this a perfectly character-integrated piece of dark comedy. Her complete amorality is the source of both horror and laughter.

jokes.text: And perhaps we will find each other along the way.
jokes.setup: George Sr. says he'll go find Buster with Lucille.
jokes.punchline: He appends a romantic cliché — 'find each other along the way' — to a practical crisis conversation, revealing his desperate attempt to use Buster's disappearance as a reconciliation gambit.
jokes.explanation: Good character moment — George Sr.'s transparent romantic maneuvering dressed up as a crisis response is very in-character. Works as mild comedy.

jokes.text: Just as I found my son, I lost him. And just as I lost my wife, I found my replacement wife, but then I lost her. I have nobody.
jokes.setup: George Sr. dramatically describes his losses.
jokes.punchline: 'Replacement wife' is the key — his unsentimental description of his second wife as a literal replacement unit undercuts the pathos, and then Maeby trying to offer comfort that he dismisses makes it worse.
jokes.explanation: The phrase 'replacement wife' is doing heavy lifting — it's a perfect George Sr. characterization. The dismissal of Maeby's comfort ('Not now, Maeby') adds a cruel punctuation.

jokes.text: Well, you got me, Dad. / Not now, Maeby. / I have... what? Like, one person.
jokes.setup: George Sr. laments he has nobody. Maeby offers comfort.
jokes.punchline: George Sr. dismisses Maeby, then immediately revises his 'nobody' to 'like, one person' — as if Maeby barely counts.
jokes.explanation: The three-beat structure is excellent: Maeby offers comfort → dismissed → grudging acknowledgment that she barely counts. The 'Like, one person' is perfectly calibrated casual cruelty.

jokes.text: Click. [Second callback to the heart switch]
jokes.setup: George Sr. dismisses Maeby.
jokes.punchline: The narrator again uses 'Click' — George Sr. has turned off his feelings for Maeby the moment she offered him warmth.
jokes.explanation: The second use of 'Click' is even more devastating than the first because this time it's directed at Maeby in the middle of an emotionally vulnerable moment. The callback is escalating in cruelty, which is excellent structural comedy writing.

jokes.text: We forgot the debt. / Basically, what we did was we absorbed everything, including you.
jokes.setup: Michael asks whether the company forgave Gob's debt.
jokes.punchline: 'We forgot the debt' instead of 'forgave the debt' — Adhir's malapropism is both accidentally accurate (they literally forgot to track it) and a pun on 'forgave/forgot.'
jokes.explanation: Elegant wordplay — 'forgot' vs. 'forgave' works on multiple levels: as a malapropism joke, as an accidentally true statement about Bluth accounting, and as a pun. Economical and smart.

jokes.text: And does Gob know that we're copresidents? / Gob doesn't know that there's a B in the word 'debt.'
jokes.setup: Michael asks if Gob is aware of their shared corporate status.
jokes.punchline: Adhir's response illustrates Gob's illiteracy/innumeracy with the specific example of not knowing there's a silent 'b' in 'debt' — and then pointedly notes that this word will come up a lot.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. The specificity of 'doesn't know there's a B in debt' is hilarious — it's concrete, it characterizes Gob instantly, and the follow-up 'if you're gonna run the Bluth Company, that word's gonna come up' is perfect structure. Highly quotable and character-specific.

jokes.text: I've got slices of stale gummy bear, hard candies and a spare rib. / Could I have the spare rib? / Just joshing. It's stale gummy bear.
jokes.setup: Tobias presents the meager attic food supply to people hiding. One person asks for the best item.
jokes.punchline: 'Just joshing' reveals there was never a spare rib — it was a cruel tease. The choice of 'joshing' (old-fashioned, theatrical Tobias word) makes it funnier.
jokes.explanation: Tobias's theatrical cruelty with 'Just joshing' is a perfect character beat. The false promise of the spare rib is a small but well-structured joke.

jokes.text: It takes a lot out of me, but they like to look at me as the sticky white glue that holds the family together.
jokes.setup: Tobias describes his role in the family.
jokes.punchline: 'Sticky white glue' is an inadvertent sexual double entendre — perfectly in keeping with Tobias's long-running pattern of accidentally saying things with gay sexual undertones.
jokes.explanation: Classic Tobias. The 'sticky white glue' is one of his better inadvertent euphemisms — specific enough to be shocking but clearly innocent in intent. The callback to seasons of similar Tobias moments adds rewatch value.

jokes.text: I promise... nay, vow that you will always have a roof over... Ow! / Roof balls!
jokes.setup: Tobias is making a grand speech about being their provider, promising them a roof over their heads — while standing in an attic.
jokes.punchline: He hits his head on the actual roof mid-speech, then shouts 'Roof balls!' — the irony of promising a roof while being hurt by one, plus 'Roof balls!' as a euphemism/exclamation.
jokes.explanation: Excellent structural joke. The setup — Tobias promising a 'roof over your heads' — directly causes the punchline: he hits his head on the roof. The physical comedy and wordplay combine perfectly. 'Roof balls!' as an exclamation is inherently funny and quotable. The structure is near-perfect: thematic, physical, and wordplay all converge.

jokes.text: Just until I can support us on my actor salary. / Oh... / Quiet. You can be excited, but quietly.
jokes.setup: Tobias promises they only have to hide until he can support them on his 'actor salary.'
jokes.punchline: The family's muffled sound of despair ('Oh...') at the prospect of waiting for Tobias's nonexistent actor income — and then Tobias misreading it as excitement — is perfectly layered.
jokes.explanation: The layered misread is great — Tobias takes their despair groan as excitement and tells them to be quietly excited. Classic Tobias obliviousness. His failed actor status is well-established so the joke lands harder with series knowledge.

jokes.text: I probably shouldn't have chained us together. / No, no, I should've brought a key.
jokes.setup: Oscar and Buster are chained together (Keystone Cops reference) trying to escape.
jokes.punchline: Oscar's admission that he shouldn't have chained them together is met by Buster's logical counter — that the real mistake was not bringing a key. Two different levels of planning failure.
jokes.explanation: Good character comedy — two well-intentioned but incompetent people layering their mistakes. The Keystone Cops framing adds absurdist dimension.

jokes.text: Keystone Cops would've made a meal out of this. / Yeah.
jokes.setup: Oscar and Buster's escape attempt is awkward and un-cinematic.
jokes.punchline: Oscar laments that their escape wasn't comedic enough — they've failed to even achieve good slapstick. The meta-observation that real physical comedy is harder than in the movies.
jokes.explanation: Nice meta-joke: they failed to be properly funny even in their attempted slapstick, and they know it. The self-aware disappointment is charming and slightly absurdist.

jokes.text: Wait a minute. It's a fake hand. How can it hurt? / No, it's a rental from prison, and Mother said my next hand is gonna be out of my own pocket.
jokes.setup: Oscar asks how Buster can feel pain in a fake hand.
jokes.punchline: The revelation that the prosthetic is a prison rental — and that Lucille has told Buster his next hand will come 'out of his own pocket' — layers absurdity (renting a prosthetic) with dark comedy (Lucille's attitude toward Buster's disability).
jokes.explanation: Brilliant layered joke. First level: the absurdity of a prison renting prosthetic hands. Second level: 'out of his own pocket' — Lucille using this idiom to mean Buster should pay literally, which is darkly funny given he has no hand/pocket. Third level: the idiom's literal meaning applies to a person with a prosthetic hand. One of the episode's best multi-layered jokes.

jokes.text: Left, right. Left, right... So we always start with left. Smooth and easy. Lemon squeezy. And... left... It wasn't a good system.
jokes.setup: Oscar and Buster devise a 'walking system' to coordinate their chained movement.
jokes.punchline: The narrator's flat 'It wasn't a good system' after the elaborate build-up perfectly deflates the sequence.
jokes.explanation: The narrator's deadpan dismissal 'It wasn't a good system' after the Rube Goldberg explanation is a great AD narrator moment. Economy of the punchline vs. the elaborate setup is well-calibrated.

jokes.text: Wha... No, I didn't know it was on. I mean, I would never purposefully run a car with the garage door closed with the family in crisis.
jokes.setup: Michael finds George Sr. near a running car in a closed garage.
jokes.punchline: George Sr.'s denial that he would 'never purposefully' do this — the word 'purposefully' doing enormous work, suggesting the act was purposeful but he wouldn't admit it.
jokes.explanation: The 'purposefully' qualifier is doing brilliant work — it's technically a denial while being an admission. Dark comedy about a suicide attempt played as slapstick misunderstanding. Character-specific to George Sr.'s self-pity.

jokes.text: You know, Dad, this is an electric car. So, the only thing you're killing is my battery.
jokes.setup: George Sr. appears to be attempting carbon monoxide poisoning in a closed garage.
jokes.punchline: Michael points out it's electric — so the only consequence is a dead battery, not a dead George.
jokes.explanation: Excellent dark joke. 'The only thing you're killing is my battery' is pitch-perfect — it's functionally a pun (battery/dead battery vs. being killed), and the deflation of a suicide attempt via electric vehicle efficiency is perfectly contemporary and perfectly deadpan Michael. Highly quotable. Close to the 8.0 anchor.

jokes.text: Is that funny? I... Oh, God, I can't get anything right.
jokes.setup: Michael quips about the electric car ruining George Sr.'s suicide attempt.
jokes.punchline: George Sr. asks if that was funny — briefly considering whether he's the butt of a joke — then collapses into self-pity. His incompetence extending even to his own self-destruction.
jokes.explanation: George Sr.'s pathetic inability to even successfully attempt suicide — and his self-aware 'I can't get anything right' — is tragicomically perfect. His character is at full expression here.

jokes.text: Meanwhile, your mother is banging every beach rat that ever dropped out of a race.
jokes.setup: George Sr. is wallowing in self-pity after the failed car incident.
jokes.punchline: Michael's unsympathetic pivot to describing Lucille's behavior, calling Dusty a 'beach rat who dropped out of a race,' is simultaneously comforting (he's with George) and more brutal.
jokes.explanation: 'Dropped out of a race' is a specific and funny description of Dusty's beach bum origins. Michael's casual delivery of this brutal information as consolation is characteristically tone-deaf.

jokes.text: Care to join me for a glass of breakfast wine?
jokes.setup: Lucille's beach rat boyfriend is introduced.
jokes.punchline: 'Breakfast wine' is the joke — wine in the morning delivered as a perfectly normal suggestion. Reveals Dusty's character instantly.
jokes.explanation: 'Breakfast wine' is two words that tell you everything about a character. Economical character introduction comedy. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: Or Uncle Oscar did, I don't know. I lost the thread on that one.
jokes.setup: Michael is trying to explain why Lucille cheated on George Sr.
jokes.punchline: His 'I lost the thread on that one' is both honest (the George/Oscar twin confusion is genuinely hard to follow) and meta — acknowledging the show's own convoluted continuity.
jokes.explanation: A gentle meta-joke acknowledging how hard it is to follow the George/Oscar storyline — even Michael, who lives it, has lost the thread. Rewards audience awareness.

jokes.text: Boy, you have to have balls of steel to survive in a hole like that. / And the same could be said for prison.
jokes.setup: Michael comments on what it takes to survive the family dynamic.
jokes.punchline: The narrator extends 'balls' to prison as well — another 'balls' callback, while also being an actual observation about Buster's situation.
jokes.explanation: Mild extension of the balls gag. The narrator's participation in the running gag adds a layer, but the joke itself is thinner than the earlier balls bits.

jokes.text: K means thousand. / Can you believe Adhir just expected me to know that? I must've told him K times, 'You gotta help me with these things.'
jokes.setup: Gob doesn't know what 'K' means in financial contexts.
jokes.punchline: Gob uses 'K' meaning 'a lot/many times' in the very same sentence where he's complaining about not knowing what K means — an inadvertent demonstration of the ambiguity.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best constructed jokes. Gob doesn't understand K=thousand, and in his complaint he says 'I must've told him K times' — using K colloquially to mean many, which actually demonstrates his own point about K being ambiguous. The ouroboros structure is masterful. Perfectly character-specific to Gob's particular brand of oblivious self-contradiction.

jokes.text: And $10,000 for a printer? / 10K. / Good Gob. Good K.
jokes.setup: Michael reacts to an expensive printer purchase.
jokes.punchline: Adhir corrects to '10K,' and Gob responds with 'Good Gob. Good K.' — as if being praised for learning, like a dog. This follows the 'I used to train dogs, so I'm very patient' line.
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback. 'Good Gob. Good K.' works because Adhir mentioned dog training, so Gob treating himself like a dog being praised ('Good boy') for learning a new word is perfectly constructed. The self-congratulatory tone is very Gob.

jokes.text: Well, to be fair, it's a 3-D printer. / Why do we need a 3-D printer? / Well, to be fair, it's a 3-D dental printer. / But they're worth twice what I got them to leave them here for... what do we care if it takes two and a half hours to print some molars?
jokes.setup: Michael questions a $10K printer expense.
jokes.punchline: Each Gob clarification makes things more absurd — from printer → 3-D printer → 3-D dental printer → 2.5 hours to print molars. The escalation of specificity while the justification gets worse is the comedy.
jokes.explanation: Classic escalation structure. Each revelation ('3-D printer → 3-D DENTAL printer → takes 2.5 hours to print MOLARS') digs a deeper hole while Gob remains confident he's making a strong case. Character-perfect oblivious escalation.

jokes.text: Can you just get me a copy of the receipt? / Hey, you want to spend a couple hundred bucks on a 3-D receipt, I'll have it printed and in your mouth before sunset.
jokes.setup: Michael asks for a paper receipt as documentation.
jokes.punchline: Gob misunderstands 'copy of the receipt' as a request to 3-D print it, extending the dental printer joke — and offers to have it 'in your mouth before sunset' like a dental procedure.
jokes.explanation: Excellent extension. The dental printer bit pays off with 'in your mouth before sunset' — which combines the dental framing with a vaguely threatening Western idiom. Gob's total commitment to the dental printer bit despite it being insane is perfect character comedy.

jokes.text: Where are you getting your capital? / Capital money.
jokes.setup: Michael questions Gob about the funding source.
jokes.punchline: Gob translates 'capital' by adding 'money' — as if that's a clarification — revealing he's heard the word but doesn't fully understand it.
jokes.explanation: Two words — 'Capital money' — perfectly encapsulate Gob's financial illiteracy while maintaining total confidence. One of the funniest lines of the episode for its sheer economy. The non-answer that proves he doesn't know what capital means.

jokes.text: Well, I can only look at the Bluth books. / The Austero books are for British eyes only, as they say. / ♪ For British eyes only ♪
jokes.setup: Adhir explains which books are accessible to whom.
jokes.punchline: Gob sings 'For British eyes only' to the tune of 'For Your Eyes Only' (James Bond), replacing 'your' with 'British' — a pun on Adhir's implied Indian/British background and the Bond film.
jokes.explanation: Gob spontaneously singing a Bond-inspired jingle about the Indian accountant is simultaneously racist (Gob), clever (Bond reference), and absurdist. The joke also functions as a character moment — Gob's oblivious casual racism mixed with his showman tendencies.

jokes.text: There's only room at this company for one copresident. What's next? You're gonna want one of my parking spaces, Michael?
jokes.setup: Michael explains he's also a copresident.
jokes.punchline: Gob's territorial outrage over Michael also being copresident, pivoting to parking space concerns — the parking space being a more tangible, petty concern that reveals Gob's actual priorities.
jokes.explanation: The pivot from corporate title to parking spaces captures Gob's priorities perfectly. He cares more about the physical perk (parking) than the abstract authority.

jokes.text: I'm just here to help. I'm not going to trump you. / Oh, well, Michael, don't worry, 'cause I already went there. / ♪ Balls in the air... ♪
jokes.setup: Michael says he won't 'trump' Gob (meaning outrank/override).
jokes.punchline: Gob says 'I already went there' and then sings something about 'balls in the air' — the Trump/balls wordplay connects to both the political figure and continuing the balls running gag.
jokes.explanation: The Trump joke and balls callback combined are somewhat forced. Works but feels like two running gags slightly awkwardly colliding rather than organic comic construction.

jokes.text: Would you be terribly offended if I offered you a candy?
jokes.setup: Michael discovers financial irregularities that implicate him. Adhir knows this is serious.
jokes.punchline: Adhir's response to the crisis is to offer candy — a completely inappropriate, conflict-avoidant gesture that reveals his character: polite to a fault even when delivering terrible news.
jokes.explanation: The candy offer in the middle of a financial revelation is perfectly timed comic deflection. Adhir's extreme politeness as conflict avoidance is a great character note delivered very economically.

jokes.text: And your mother has my passport, so why don't we say that I was never here? An absolute professional pleasure to have met you.
jokes.setup: Adhir realizes Michael knows too much. He wants to exit.
jokes.punchline: His extremely formal 'absolute professional pleasure to have met you' as he tries to disappear is high-comedy — the most proper possible way to exit a criminal conspiracy.
jokes.explanation: The formality of 'absolute professional pleasure to have met you' while trying to disappear from a crime scene is perfectly absurdist and character-consistent. Adhir's British-corporate politeness as a getaway strategy is genuinely funny.

jokes.text: You know, the easy choice would be to quit. / There it is. / We've got an opportunity to save the company... / I quit.
jokes.setup: Michael is giving a speech about making the hard choice to stay and save the company.
jokes.punchline: Gob hears only 'quit' from 'the easy choice would be to quit' — and immediately quits, not waiting for the rest of the speech.
jokes.explanation: Structurally brilliant. Michael's speech is designed to lead away from quitting, but Gob clocks 'quit' and acts on it immediately — before the rhetorical turn. The 'I shouldn't have called it the easy choice' kicker is perfect. Gob as someone who only hears what he wants is perfectly character-integrated. One of the episode's strongest jokes.

jokes.text: They think I'm too stupid to... [pause, candy] Help yourself. / Yeah. You know what's funny? They think that I'm too stup... [longer pause, still eating candy]
jokes.setup: Gob is about to deliver a triumphant speech about proving people wrong about his intelligence
jokes.punchline: He interrupts his own speech twice to deal with the candy — the physical comedy of the candy undercutting his moment of resolve
jokes.explanation: The visual comedy of Gob being undone by a candy in the middle of his 'I'll show them!' moment is well-constructed. The repeated self-interruption escalates perfectly. 'I already had one in there, so...' is a lovely detail.

jokes.text: I shouldn't have called it the easy choice.
jokes.setup: Gob has quit after Michael mentioned quitting as 'the easy choice.'
jokes.punchline: Michael's rueful 'I shouldn't have called it the easy choice' is the perfect coda — acknowledging his rhetorical mistake with extreme understatement.
jokes.explanation: The perfect button on the Gob-quits joke. Michael's complete deadpan acknowledgment of his own rhetorical error is quintessential straight-man comedy. Eight words that land perfectly.

jokes.text: Now, we cannot draw attention to ourselves. / I'm just a guy fixing a broken hand. / Right. And I'm... I'm just a guy who's wearing a blue hat. / Nothing to see here, folks.
jokes.setup: Oscar and Buster, fugitives with a prosthetic hand issue, try to be inconspicuous.
jokes.punchline: Oscar's disguise is fixing a prosthetic hand (extremely distinctive), and his own disguise is... a blue hat. The elaborate seriousness applied to the lamest possible cover stories.
jokes.explanation: The deflation from 'broken hand' to 'blue hat' as equivalent disguises is funny. Oscar's complete confidence in 'I'm just a guy wearing a blue hat' as an adequate cover story captures his hippie spaciness.

jokes.text: I think I saw William Shatner wearing this while riding a bicycle once.
jokes.setup: Oscar is browsing prosthetic hands in the hospital lab.
jokes.punchline: William Shatner in a prosthetic hand on a bicycle is a completely random and specific image that is funny purely for its absurd specificity.
jokes.explanation: A low-key absurdist non-sequitur. William Shatner reference works but doesn't quite justify itself beyond random celebrity specificity. Mild laugh.

jokes.text: It sort of reminds me of Mother's hand, except it's less veiny and warmer.
jokes.setup: Buster is trying on a prosthetic hand in the hospital.
jokes.punchline: Buster comparing the prosthetic favorably to his mother's actual hand is deeply Oedipal and disturbing, while being played as a completely innocent observation.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best character jokes. Buster's comparison of a prosthetic to his mother's hand — as a compliment to the prosthetic — is quintessential Buster: Oedipal, disturbing, and utterly sincere. 'Less veiny and warmer' are specific details that make it funnier. Perfect character integration.

jokes.text: Meanwhile, Gob was preparing to walk out of a job he'd put over eight hours of his life into.
jokes.setup: Gob is making a dramatic exit from the company.
jokes.punchline: 'Over eight hours of his life' quantifies Gob's total investment in this job — and it's pathetically small, delivered as if it's a significant sacrifice.
jokes.explanation: The narrator's 'eight hours of his life' is perfectly calibrated understatement. The specificity (not 'a week' or 'a few days' but 'eight hours') is exact enough to be devastating. Gob's work ethic distilled to a number.

jokes.text: This is just as tough for you as it is for me. Tougher, maybe. Man without a king.
jokes.setup: Gob is saying goodbye to... Michael? The office? His chair?
jokes.punchline: 'Man without a king' as a dramatic self-description — a reference to his magician persona — implies Gob sees himself as royalty even while being fired/quitting a job he barely did.
jokes.explanation: Gob's theatrical self-aggrandizement in a deflating moment is in-character. 'Man without a king' is slightly obscure but feels Gob-appropriate.

jokes.text: I'm gonna miss this view. / Rite Aid roof as far as you can see.
jokes.setup: Gob says a dramatic farewell to his view from the office.
jokes.punchline: The 'view' is nothing but a Rite Aid rooftop. Gob's genuine sentimentality about the most mundane possible view is the joke.
jokes.explanation: The specificity of 'Rite Aid roof as far as you can see' is wonderfully specific and deflating. Gob's genuine emotion about a parking lot drug store roof is very funny, and the image (Gob wistfully staring at a Rite Aid) is a great visual implied by the dialogue.

jokes.text: He should've thought of that before he fired me. / You do know that Michael didn't fire you, you quit. / As any proud gay man would do in the face of such bigotry.
jokes.setup: Gob is rewriting the narrative of his departure.
jokes.punchline: Adhir correctly points out Gob quit — and Gob's response is to immediately recast it as a protest resignation against bigotry, with zero basis.
jokes.explanation: Gob's immediate narrative revision — from 'quitting' to 'heroic protest against bigotry' — and Adhir's calm factual correction, is a great character exchange. Gob's theatrical victimhood is very in-character.

jokes.text: But I guess I don't need to tell that to a little Indian guy, huh? Michael's not your friend either. / You should've seen the look on his face when I do my impression of you. You know... 'What's the problem with Apu?'
jokes.setup: Gob tries to bond with Adhir over shared mistreatment by Michael.
jokes.punchline: His attempted solidarity pivots immediately to a racist impression — 'What's the problem with Apu?' — which is Gob doing an impression of Michael doing a racist impression, revealing that Gob himself is casually racist.
jokes.explanation: Gob's casual racism while attempting to ally himself with Adhir is character-perfect cringe comedy. The layers (bonding attempt → racist impression → self-unaware delivery) are well-constructed. The Apu/Simpsons reference adds a meta-dimension.

jokes.text: Just one more thing before I have security drag you out. Why are you claiming that the closet conversion company was a religious write-off? / Because I went there to become straight.
jokes.setup: Adhir tries to process the books before Gob leaves.
jokes.punchline: Gob's completely earnest explanation of claiming a gay conversion therapy company as a religious write-off reveals both his financial illiteracy and his muddled grasp of the conversion therapy concept.
jokes.explanation: The financial and spiritual confusion combined in one answer ('I went there to become straight') is very Gob. It explains the write-off while revealing his ongoing sexual identity crisis.

jokes.text: Those places don't work. There's no such thing as a program that can change someone. It's a way for hypocrites to try to convince the world that they're now straight. / Without using magic.
jokes.setup: Adhir gives a thoughtful explanation of why conversion therapy doesn't work.
jokes.punchline: Gob's addition of 'Without using magic' treats magic as the one legitimate alternative to conversion therapy — completely missing Adhir's point while also revealing Gob's magical thinking about his own sexuality.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. Adhir's complete, thoughtful summary of why conversion therapy fails is punctuated by Gob's 'Without using magic' — which simultaneously: (1) misses the point entirely, (2) reveals Gob believes magic could achieve what therapy cannot, (3) references his own identity as a magician, and (4) is darkly funny about his denial. Economy is exceptional — three words do enormous work. Close to the Snip-snap calibration level for character integration.

jokes.text: So I just tell the world that I'm gay, and that I'm going to one of those? And then I come out straight.
jokes.setup: Gob has a plan to manage his public image.
jokes.punchline: His plan — announce he's gay, attend conversion therapy, 'come out straight' — is exactly backwards from how conversion therapy is typically discussed, and also reveals his magical thinking about the process.
jokes.explanation: Gob's perfectly backwards logic is well-constructed. He has it exactly right structurally (come out as gay → go to conversion → come out 'straight') but for entirely wrong reasons and with delusional confidence.

jokes.text: I didn't understand a word you just said, little guy.
jokes.setup: Adhir has explained why Gob's plan is misguided.
jokes.punchline: Gob's complete dismissal — 'I didn't understand a word you just said, little guy' — is an admission of ignorance combined with condescension, revealing that Gob tuned out after 'magic.'
jokes.explanation: The casual dismissal 'little guy' added to a genuine admission of incomprehension is a perfect Gob exit line. It's simultaneously rude and self-aware (he genuinely didn't understand). Economy is excellent.

jokes.text: You know Betty White has this same car? / You know Betty White? / We share a weed dealer.
jokes.setup: Rebel makes a casual comment about Betty White having the same car.
jokes.punchline: George Michael asks how she knows — and Rebel's answer is that they share a weed dealer, a funny subversion of how you might normally connect to a TV legend.
jokes.explanation: Clean three-beat setup/question/punchline. 'We share a weed dealer' is economical, unexpected, and very funny. The image of Betty White's weed dealer network is genuinely amusing. High quotability.

jokes.text: We are Anonymous. / Oh, shoot, not again.
jokes.setup: The Guy Fawkes masked protesters reappear.
jokes.punchline: George Michael's 'Oh, shoot, not again' — mild expletive, exhausted resignation — treats the scary hacker collective like a minor annoyance.
jokes.explanation: George Michael's resigned 'not again' perfectly deflates the menace of Anonymous. His mild 'shoot' as expletive is very George Michael. The gag continues to run well.

jokes.text: Online hacking I can deal with, it's just the day that they show up in person that I'm dreading. / But you can't live in fear, so... let's go. Whatever happens, happens.
jokes.setup: George Michael is explaining his fear of Anonymous showing up in person.
jokes.punchline: Rebel cheerfully says 'you can't live in fear' and immediately walks into the Anonymous protest — which is exactly the thing George Michael was dreading, happening immediately.
jokes.explanation: The immediate ironic fulfillment of the thing George Michael just said he dreaded — delivered cheerfully by Rebel — is a clean structural joke. Good economy.

jokes.text: Oscar and Buster encounter Stan Sitwell in the hospital — Stan immediately says 'Please don't hurt me.'
jokes.setup: Oscar (who looks exactly like George Bluth, Sitwell's nemesis) and Buster (who just escaped prison) run into Stan Sitwell.
jokes.punchline: Stan's immediate 'Please don't hurt me' — pre-emptive surrender to the apparently threatening Bluths — is a great reaction beat given the comedy of who they actually are.
jokes.explanation: Decent callback moment. Stan's fear is reasonable given context, and 'Please don't hurt me' is a good deflation of his normally pompous character.

jokes.text: Could we stay at your place? / You're taking me as a hostage in case the cops see us? / Hey, guy, I wouldn't know about that. / We can pretend we're cousins.
jokes.setup: Oscar and Buster ask Sitwell if they can stay at his place.
jokes.punchline: Sitwell concludes he's being taken hostage. Oscar's denial — 'Hey, guy, I wouldn't know about that' — is itself suspicious, and then Buster's suggestion to 'pretend we're cousins' is an adorably naïve alternative.
jokes.explanation: The 'pretend we're cousins' solution is very Buster — childlike innocence as a getaway strategy. Oscar's conspicuous denial adds to the comedy.

jokes.text: Right behind you, cousin-father.
jokes.setup: Buster is going along with Sitwell and Oscar.
jokes.punchline: 'Cousin-father' — Buster acknowledges that Oscar is both his cousin (twin of George Sr.) and potentially his actual father, in one hyphenated term, delivered completely naturally.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's very best jokes. 'Cousin-father' is genius — it's two words that carry enormous series-long baggage, delivered by Buster with complete casual naturalness. It rewards long-term viewers, it's darkly funny about the family's dysfunction, and it's extremely quotable. The economy of the hyphenate is perfect. This is close to the 9-10 tier for being a genuinely great AD joke that will be quoted by fans.

jokes.text: You hired a bunch of foxy guys to protest me because I said I hated hot guys. / 'Rebel.' 'Unfair.' / I was reading that as rebel.
jokes.setup: George Michael sees the protest signs that say 'REBEL' and 'UNFAIR.'
jokes.punchline: He misreads 'REBEL' (the name of his girlfriend) as 'rebel' (noun/adjective), and thought the protest was about him hating hot guys — which is itself wrong for multiple reasons.
jokes.explanation: Excellent layered AD-style confusion. George Michael misread the protest signs in multiple ways simultaneously — and his misreading (they're protesting his opinion of hot guys) is itself based on the cascading misunderstandings of the episode. The payoff uses the girlfriend's name as the sign's text. Well-constructed.

jokes.text: I went as you for Halloween last year. / Yes, I went as you for Halloween last year. / Can we get a picture?
jokes.setup: The protesters (supposed Anonymous hackers) recognize Rebel Alley as a celebrity.
jokes.punchline: The fearsome Anonymous members immediately turn into starstruck fans wanting selfies and confessing they dressed as Rebel for Halloween — complete tonal reversal.
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of the Anonymous-as-dorks bit. The Halloween costume admission is the best detail — these supposedly fearsome hackers are celebrity gossip fans.

jokes.text: Oh, my goodness. Fakeblock is so impressive. / Yeah. / I mean, Fakeblock's incredible. / Fakeblock is... / Yeah, Fakeblock is real.
jokes.setup: George Michael brought Rebel to Fakeblock expecting it to be empty — but it turns out to be fully operational.
jokes.punchline: George Michael's stunned affirmations — 'Yeah... Yeah, Fakeblock is real' — reveal his genuine surprise that his fake company apparently exists. He's the last to know his lie became true.
jokes.explanation: Season-long payoff. The irony of George Michael — who knows Fakeblock is fake — being surprised to discover it's real is an excellent structural reversal. His 'Yeah, Fakeblock is real' is delivered with the dazed quality of someone whose lie has escaped them. A genuine AD-style payoff that rewards long-term viewing.

jokes.text: Lucille, my lady, my love. Do you remember our first date, at that little Italian restaurant? / ♪ How did I ever find you? ♪ / You took me for Chinese. The Italian place was the first time I caught you with your secretary, and you asked, 'How did you ever find me?'
jokes.setup: George Sr. attempts a romantic speech about their first date at an Italian restaurant, singing a love song.
jokes.punchline: Lucille corrects every detail: it was Chinese, not Italian — and the Italian restaurant was actually where she caught him cheating, and the song was his panicked question 'How did you EVER find ME?' (not a love song).
jokes.explanation: Exceptional joke. George Sr.'s romanticized memory is systematically dismantled by Lucille: wrong restaurant, wrong song, wrong context. The punchline — the 'love song' was actually his panicked reaction to being caught cheating — is a complete inversion. The structural precision (setup expects romance, delivers infidelity) and the character integration (both characters are perfect) make this one of the episode's best moments. The song transition ♪ How did you ever find me ♪ is the perfect payoff.

jokes.text: That's it. All right. Get out. Go back to the Macaroni Grill.
jokes.setup: After the romanticized memory collapses, Lucille dismisses George Sr.
jokes.punchline: 'Go back to the Macaroni Grill' — a callback to the earlier Macaroni Grill joke, and the specificity of Macaroni Grill as a downgrade from the Italian restaurant narrative is perfectly deflating.
jokes.explanation: Good callback button on the scene. Macaroni Grill as the anti-romantic destination for George Sr. is a well-established running joke here. The specificity of the chain restaurant name is always funny.

jokes.text: I want you, Lucille. / In what way, George? / Sexually? / Yes. / Sexual-ish-ly.
jokes.setup: George Sr. says he wants Lucille back.
jokes.punchline: Lucille asks him to specify 'in what way' — and his 'Yes. Sexual-ish-ly' hedges his answer with 'ish' — suggesting his desire is somewhat less than fully committed, which is itself the problem.
jokes.explanation: Excellent joke. The word 'Sexual-ish-ly' is the punchline — the '-ish' suffix applied to sexuality is funny in itself, and reveals George Sr.'s half-heartedness about even his own desires. Highly quotable neologism. Lucille's 'In what way, George? Sexually?' as a straight clarifying question adds the right setup.

jokes.text: Look, here's the deal. I can't... get hard. I... / Live without me. / It's really getting hard, uh, to live without you.
jokes.setup: George Sr. starts a sentence implying erectile dysfunction ('I can't get hard').
jokes.punchline: Lucille completes it as 'live without me' — and George Sr. recovers with 'It's really getting hard to live without you,' making the initial implication of ED into a metaphor for loneliness, while also being a pun on 'hard.'
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best constructed jokes. Three-beat structure: (1) George implies ED, (2) Lucille completes it romantically, (3) George adopts her completion as his own sentence while keeping the double meaning active. The resulting 'It's really getting hard to live without you' works as both romantic and crude simultaneously. Perfect economy, perfect structure. This is AD joke-writing at its best — layered, character-specific, and quotable.

jokes.text: He didn't say a word until we fired the wet nurse. / He didn't stop talking about that for three years. / Until we caved.
jokes.setup: George Sr. and Lucille reminisce about Buster's development
jokes.punchline: Buster's first words were about the wet nurse dismissal; he talked about it for three years until they 'caved' and presumably rehired her — the co-dependency is established at birth
jokes.explanation: A brilliant compressed backstory joke — Buster's entire psychological profile (trauma, fixation, dependency, manipulation) is conveyed in three sentences. 'Until we caved' is the devastating button. Perfect character integration for the whole Bluth family system.

jokes.text: He better not have started talking. / No, I meant originally. Like, when he was three. / Five. / Five. / He didn't say a word until we fired the wet nurse. / He didn't stop talking about that for three years. / Until we caved.
jokes.setup: George Sr. nostalgically recalls when Buster first started talking.
jokes.punchline: The entire mini-story: Buster didn't speak until age 5 (they got the age wrong), he only started talking when they fired the wet nurse, talked about nothing else for three years, and they 'caved' and rehired her.
jokes.explanation: Multiple jokes stacked in quick succession. Lucille's 'He better not have started talking' (worried he's confessing), corrected age, the wet nurse detail (Buster's arrested development triggered by losing his surrogate mother), three years of complaints, and them caving — each beat is a small joke that builds a complete picture of Buster's psychology and the family's dysfunction. A sustained excellence.

jokes.text: That makes life worth living? / I was actually gonna say that we should keep from the DA, but can you tell me why life is worth living again?
jokes.setup: George Sr. says 'this is the stuff' — implying the family memories are what make life worth living.
jokes.punchline: Lucille misunderstands him as cataloging reasons for life; George then reveals he meant 'stuff to keep from the DA' — their shared tender moment was actually a list of incriminating evidence.
jokes.explanation: Beautifully dark. A genuinely tender moment between George and Lucille — sharing memories, finding connection — is revealed to be a list of things to hide from prosecutors. The pivot 'I was actually gonna say we should keep from the DA' is devastating and funny. Pure AD: sentiment and criminality are indistinguishable.

jokes.text: He loves music, he plays me songs on the guitar. You know, hey, I just brought the assistant manager of the Macaroni Grill over here during his lunch shift. It set me back a sawbuck. It's a third of what you are giving me to live off these days.
jokes.setup: Lucille compares Dusty favorably to George Sr.
jokes.punchline: George Sr.'s counter is that he hired an assistant manager from Macaroni Grill to deliver food. His 'it set me back a sawbuck' (a $10 bill) as his romantic gesture is a masterclass in pathetic undercutting.
jokes.explanation: The specificity of 'a sawbuck' (old-fashioned $10 term George Sr. would use) for his romantic gesture is perfect character comedy. The Macaroni Grill callback lands again.

jokes.text: Yeah, that's another thing Dusty doesn't complain about, his allowance. / You give him an allowance? / He doesn't get one. He's very wealthy. He made a lot of money in his law career. And he's fun. He surfs.
jokes.setup: Lucille brags about Dusty not complaining about his allowance.
jokes.punchline: George Sr. assumes Lucille gives Dusty an allowance — but she reveals Dusty doesn't get one because he's wealthy. The reversal (Lucille who is controlling about money is with a man who doesn't need her money) undercuts George Sr.'s assumption.
jokes.explanation: The misdirect on 'allowance' is good but not exceptional. The detail that Dusty was a lawyer who now surfs is a good comic character sketch.

jokes.text: I'm alive. / Only because Michael drives an electric car.
jokes.setup: George Sr. tries to claim he's 'alive' in the vibrant sense Lucille means.
jokes.punchline: Lucille's immediate callback to the electric car suicide attempt — 'Only because Michael drives an electric car' — is delivered as a factual rebuttal, not a joke.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. The callback to the electric car garage scene is devastating — delivered by Lucille as a flat, factual statement with zero sympathy. The economy is near-perfect. The dark comedy of George Sr.'s attempted suicide being deflated by the eco-friendliness of Michael's car choice is a running bit that pays off perfectly here.

jokes.text: Trust me, the whole 'trying to kill yourself' thing, turn off.
jokes.setup: George Sr. is trying to win Lucille back.
jokes.punchline: Lucille gives romantic advice: don't try to kill yourself, it's not attractive. Delivered with complete pragmatic authority as if this is normal dating advice.
jokes.explanation: Lucille's dating tip 'the whole trying to kill yourself thing, turn off' is perfectly in character — she's not comforting him, she's critiquing his romantic strategy. The delivery implies she's seen this before and is bored by it. Great character integration.

jokes.text: I think I need to show you that I am a fun guy who likes to laugh and is open to not killing himself.
jokes.setup: George Sr. decides to win Lucille back by showing he's fun.
jokes.punchline: 'And is open to not killing himself' — George Sr. frames 'not committing suicide' as a selling point in his pitch, as if it's an amenity rather than a baseline.
jokes.explanation: Excellent. 'Open to not killing himself' as a romantic selling point is darkly absurd and very funny. The normalization of suicide attempt as one quality among many ('fun, likes to laugh, and doesn't kill himself') is pitch-black comedy delivered with complete Bluth earnestness.

jokes.text: I think I need to show you that I am a fun guy who likes to laugh and is open to not killing himself.
jokes.setup: George Sr. resolves to win Lucille back by being more fun
jokes.punchline: His resolution includes 'open to not killing himself' as a selling point — presented as a reasonable new personality trait he's adding
jokes.explanation: 'Open to not killing himself' as a self-improvement goal is a wonderfully dark character beat — George Sr. treats his own mortality as a preference he's willing to reconsider for romantic reasons. Very AD.

jokes.text: I don't call it that, I call it 'a cry for help,' but I think help has just arrived and it's saying, 'Cry for fun!' / Don't cry at all. / Don't cry. Don't die. And have some fun.
jokes.setup: George Sr. reframes his suicide attempt as a 'cry for help' and proposes a new mantra.
jokes.punchline: The evolution from 'cry for help' → 'cry for fun!' → Lucille's 'Don't cry at all' → the final condensed mantra 'Don't cry. Don't die. And have some fun' — the mantra escalates to include not dying as a fun activity.
jokes.explanation: The mantra 'Don't cry. Don't die. And have some fun.' is beautifully absurd — it rhymes, it escalates, and it treats 'don't die' as an aspirational goal. The three-beat structure builds naturally and lands a genuinely quotable phrase. One of the episode's most memorable moments.

jokes.text: Hurt people, hurt people.
jokes.setup: The scene ends with George Sr. departing after the emotional exchange.
jokes.punchline: 'Hurt people, hurt people' — a play on the self-help saying 'Hurt people hurt people' (damaged people damage others), but here it could also mean 'hurt people [comma] hurt people' — the imperative to harm. The ambiguity is the joke.
jokes.explanation: Three words with genuine grammatical ambiguity: the self-help aphorism ('hurt people hurt people' = damaged people damage others) vs. the imperative ('hurt people, hurt people' = go injure people). The Bluth family perfectly embodies both readings. Exceptional economy — perfect for the AD narrator. One of the episode's cleverest moments.

jokes.text: NEXT: George Sr. at the beach — 'I would like an hour on the funnest thing you have.' / 'We got a tandem bike.' / 'Unless you're alone, then it's the saddest thing.' / 'Second-funnest thing you have.' / 'We got the ball.'
jokes.setup: George Sr. asks for the 'funnest thing' at the beach rental stand.
jokes.punchline: The vendor reveals the tandem bike is 'the saddest thing' for a lone rider — so George Sr. asks for the second-funnest thing, and gets a ball. The escalating deflation.
jokes.explanation: The tandem bike/saddest thing for a lone rider is a brilliant joke — something designed for two becomes a symbol of isolation. 'Second-funnest thing' escalating to just a ball is perfect deflation. One of the stinger's best moments.

jokes.text: And soon, finds himself having a ball. / Don't cry, don't die. Don't cry, don't die. / Help me. / Don't die! / It sounds like he said, 'Don't help me.' / I heard, 'Help me die.' / Either way, it looks like he's having fun.
jokes.setup: George Sr. is on a large beach ball, chanting the mantra, and appears to be in trouble.
jokes.punchline: The 'having a ball' pun (literal ball + having fun), the mantra being used as desperate cries, and bystanders mishearing 'help me' as 'don't help me' or 'help me die' — while declaring it looks like fun.
jokes.explanation: Excellent stinger that ties together multiple running threads: the ball gag (literally), the mantra, and the suicide theme. The bystanders mishearing 'help me' as 'help me die' while judging he looks fine is dark comedy at its best. The narrator's 'having a ball' pun is perfect closure.

jokes.text: Anyway, that's why I came here. I'm ready to convert. / Our church runs a program that sees confused men through a very difficult time. / Perfect. Sign me up for that. / This isn't one of those places where, like, they get you to quit smoking by making you smoke tons of cigarettes until you're like, 'Oh, no, no more...' You know what I mean? / I don't want you to think I'm a whore. / No one would suggest that. Um, shall we get on our knees? / Oh, are we starting?
jokes.setup: Gob arrives at a conversion therapy program
jokes.punchline: The smoking metaphor for 'conversion by overload' implies Gob thinks they'll cure him by having a lot of gay sex; 'I don't want you to think I'm a whore' followed by 'shall we get on our knees?' / 'Oh, are we starting?' — Gob misinterprets prayer as sexual
jokes.explanation: The scene packs three escalating Gob jokes: the smoking metaphor revealing his assumption about how conversion works, 'I don't want you to think I'm a whore' as an actually relevant concern he voices inappropriately, and 'Oh, are we starting?' misreading prayer as sex. Each beat is character-perfectly Gob — his repression, his obliviousness, his magical thinking. The escalation is tight and the final punchline is the strongest. Excellent end-tag comedy.

jokes.text: Gob at the conversion therapy church: 'This isn't one of those places where, like, they get you to quit smoking by making you smoke tons of cigarettes until you're like, Oh, no, no more...' / 'I don't want you to think I'm a whore.' / 'No.' / 'Shall we get on our knees?' / 'Oh, are we starting?'
jokes.setup: Gob arrives at the conversion therapy church.
jokes.punchline: Three stacked jokes: (1) Gob's bizarre cigarette-aversion therapy analogy, (2) 'I don't want you to think I'm a whore,' (3) His eager misinterpretation of 'shall we get on our knees?' as a sexual invitation.
jokes.explanation: A strong stinger triptych. Gob's conversion therapy confusion: the cigarette analogy (Gob's logic about aversion therapy is both wrong and makes its own kind of sense), 'I don't want you to think I'm a whore' (pure Gob), and 'Oh, are we starting?' to what is clearly a prayer position — reveal Gob's complete inability to separate sexuality from everything. Perfectly character-integrated escalation.

tmdb_overview: Buster fails to show for his own prison release party. Michael examines company financial records. George-Michael seeks Anonymous help to deceive Rebel.
insight: Wordplay and character quirks sustain a mid-tier episode struggling for impact momentum.
